reply_content:
I don't think I concur with that thought. The new Coach makes a big difference even if the team was inherited from the previous coach. 
To give you an example, David Moyes inherited SAF's Man Utd. in 2013. On paper, Man Utd. should have flourished under Moyes because of how great they were the previous seasons. But that wasn't the case, was it? Man Utd. also had the privilege to sign top players like Di Maria, Falcao, Pogba, Zlatan, even the great CR7. But did these players last longer than 3 years at Man Utd.? NO, they did not. 
My point here is that the coach plays a critical role in setting up the culture and the mentality of the players. Even if the players were made by the previous coach, the new coach has to maintain the consistency and resilience of these players. It is unfair to Slot to discredit him of the success of the previous season.
The reason why we are struggling now is because half the team is new, its taking time of them to click in. Slot has to work to restructure our tactics that fit well with these new players.

author: @PhrygianTurk

reply_content:
Why is nobody questioning Liverpool’s sporting directors? There are two of them, yet for years they haven’t addressed the obvious defensive crisis. Since spending €85M on Van Dijk, they haven’t even tried to rebuild the back line. Modern football demands young, powerful, muscular defenders—Guéhi was available for €35–45M, a bargain compared to today’s market. Instead, the club spent around €250M on two extremely fragile forwards, Ekitike and Isak.

Recent recruitment has been dreadful. Every signing looks physically weak—talented, but soft. Liverpool need leaders with presence, height, muscle, and charisma. Just look at how slim Isak, Ekitike, and Wirtz are. Kerkez and Tsimikas get overpowered far too easily. Meanwhile, the club ignores affordable, top-quality options like Olise or PSG’s Nuno Mendes. This all points to one thing: Richard Hughes and Barry Hunter are not doing their jobs properly.

Liverpool also remain far too dependent on Salah. He should have been sold to Saudi Arabia in the summer—the timing was perfect. He can’t dribble or accelerate like he used to, and at this stage he should focus on being a creator for Ekitike and Isak. Gakpo is becoming as unreliable as Darwin Núñez; either he becomes a playmaker or he sits.

Wirtz needs courage, arrogance, and leadership—he plays like a shy schoolboy. Mac Allister disappears far too often. The best players right now are Szoboszlai, Gravenberch, and Bradley. Robertson still has heart and power, but he’s losing agility. Chiesa has character but lacks finesse and a consistent strike; maybe he needs to play closer to goal, where his chaos can cause problems.

Ekitike has everything except physical strength, and he’s not a team player yet—when he scores, he celebrates like a solo hero instead of thanking the teammate who created the chance. And Isak? The way he forced his exit from Newcastle shows he could do the same to Liverpool. Now that he got his move, he must work like a machine and deliver.

And none of this should be coming from fans. The manager should be the first to see these issues. Slot inherited Klopp’s hard work—last season’s success was purely Klopp’s legacy. If Slot can’t build something stable out of this chaos, he should make way for Glasner. Glasner would fix the defence immediately, and he’d do it without overspending.

Liverpool must also replace their sporting director and head of scouting. Since Jota, their transfer record has been one failure after another.
